title: Unforeseen high continental-scale soil microbiome homogenization in urban greenspaces

data_scope.exact_sample_count: not stated in abstract (defined in Methods/Supplementary)

core_conclusion: Across 13 Chinese cities and four land uses, urban greenspaces host higher local (alpha) richness of soil bacteria, protists and fungi (but not metazoa) than farmlands and forests, an effect closely tied to higher soil pH; yet at the continental scale urban communities are more homogeneous (lower beta diversity) than farmland communities. Urbanization thus drives biotic homogenization and more synchronous responses to environmental stressors, a hidden risk for the resilience of city soils.
why_it_matters: Reframes higher urban soil richness as a warning rather than a win: locally richer but regionally homogenized communities respond synchronously to stressors, raising systemic risk for soil function in cities. Uses adjacent farmland/forest baselines to isolate the urban signal and identifies soil pH as a tractable management lever.
why_nature: Continental-scale, multi-city design seeking a transferable urbanization signature rather than a single case; counter-intuitive yet mechanistic result (locally rich, regionally homogenized, driven by pH); cross-kingdom whole-community scope; directly policy-translatable to greenspace and soil-resilience planning.
added_value_to_my_research: Spatially explicit beta-diversity surface via explainable ML / MGWR -> homogenization risk mapping
Microbiome-health coupling -> quality-weighted greenspace accessibility indicator
Urbanization-age chronosequence -> spatiotemporal trajectory modeling of homogenization
Field-to-remote-sensing upscaling -> multi-source fusion and geospatial foundation-model fine-tuning
Correlated-response risk model -> efficiency-equity-resilience framing for greenspace planning
